findings.explanation: HLD_AWS_193 already flags `authorization = "NONE"` on this method, but the config pairs it with `api_key_required = true`, which looks like a compensating control to a human reader and would suppress a naive re-check. It isn't one: AWS API keys are explicitly not an authentication or authorization mechanism (they exist for usage-plan throttling/billing only) and don't validate caller identity. Worse, browsers cannot attach custom headers like `x-api-key` to a CORS preflight request, so enforcing `api_key_required` on an OPTIONS method will make real cross-origin preflights fail with 403 — the control is simultaneously ineffective as security and actively breaks the CORS flow it sits next to. Because this method is backed by a MOCK integration with no business logic, `authorization = "NONE"` is actually the correct choice here; the defect is specifically the false sense of protection from `api_key_required`.
findings.remediation: Remove `api_key_required = true` from the OPTIONS method. Keep `authorization = "NONE"` (appropriate for a MOCK-backed CORS preflight) and continue to rely on `messages_method`'s `authorization = var.authorization` (default `AWS_IAM`) for the actual access control on the functional route.
findings.title: api_key_required on the OPTIONS method is not an authorization control and breaks CORS preflight

findings.explanation: HLD_AWS_143 already flags the wildcard principal on this statement, and per the review taxonomy the engine can't evaluate whether the `IpAddress` condition on `var.allowed_range` actually narrows access at runtime — a classic Condition-block blind spot. Looking at the resolved value: `var.allowed_range`'s only validation checks that entries are non-empty strings (variables.tf:22-30); nothing rejects `0.0.0.0/0` or `::/0`. Combined with `var.authorization` (variables.tf:32-41), which defaults to `AWS_IAM` but accepts `"NONE"`, a caller who sets `allowed_range = ["0.0.0.0/0"]` and `authorization = "NONE"` gets a fully public, unauthenticated Lambda-backed POST endpoint with no error or warning from the module — both example configs in this repo pass safe private CIDRs, but nothing in the module enforces that at the interface boundary.
findings.title: Wildcard-principal resource policy's IP condition has no floor — nothing stops allowed_range from being 0.0.0.0/0

findings.explanation: `aws_api_gateway_client_certificate.pike` is created and wired to the stage via `client_certificate_id`, which reads as an mTLS-style backend-authentication control. API Gateway only presents this certificate on outbound calls to an `HTTP`/`HTTP_PROXY` integration so the backend can validate the gateway's identity. Every integration this module defines is `type = "AWS"` (Lambda, aws_api_gateway_integration.messages_integration.tf:5) or `type = "MOCK"` (aws_api_gateway_integration.mock.tf:5) — neither uses or validates a client certificate. This is dead configuration: it costs nothing to fail, but it also gives reviewers false confidence that backend calls are certificate-authenticated when no such verification is actually possible with a Lambda integration. No rule flags this because it requires correlating the stage's `client_certificate_id` against every integration `type` on the same REST API.
findings.remediation: Remove `aws_api_gateway_client_certificate.pike` and the `client_certificate_id` attribute on the stage unless/until this module adds an `HTTP_PROXY` integration to a backend that actually validates the client certificate. If it's intentionally reserved for future HTTP backends, document that explicitly next to the resource.
findings.title: Client certificate attached to the stage has no effect — the only backend integration is Lambda, not HTTP_PROXY

findings.explanation: `var.kms_key_id`'s validation explicitly allows `""` (`var.kms_key_id == "" || length(trim(var.kms_key_id)) > 0`), and that value flows straight into `aws_cloudwatch_log_group.api_gateway_execution_logs.kms_key_id` with no further check. HLD_AWS_039 (CloudWatch log groups must be encrypted with a KMS key) doesn't fire because holden sees a variable reference, not a literal, and treats it as satisfying `not_null` — a documented variable/expression blind spot. Both example configurations in this repo pass a real KMS key ARN, so the gap isn't exercised today, but the module's own contract permits any consumer to pass `kms_key_id = ""` and silently lose log encryption (or hit an opaque AWS API error, depending on provider version, rather than a clear Terraform-time failure).
findings.remediation: Drop the empty-string branch from the validation and require a real key ARN, or make the opt-out explicit and provider-safe: change `kms_key_id` to `type = string`, `default = null`, and pass `null` (not `""`) through to the log group so an absent key correctly falls back to encryption-at-rest with the AWS-managed default instead of sending an invalid empty string to the API.
findings.title: kms_key_id validation permits an empty string, letting callers silently disable log group encryption

suggested_policies.rationale: aws_api_gateway_usage_plan has zero deterministic coverage today (coverage_by_type is empty for this type). 'Has an API key' is frequently conflated with 'is rate-limited', but this module ties a usage plan to the stage (aws_api_gateway_usage_plan.api.tf) without a throttle_settings block, so a valid API key has no burst or steady-state ceiling — a leaked or shared key enables unbounded cost amplification or an application-layer DoS. This is a plain attribute-presence check on one resource type with no context/intent dependency, so it belongs in the deterministic engine rather than being re-discovered by AI on every scan.
suggested_policies.yaml:
id: HLD_PROPOSED_001
name: API Gateway usage plans must define throttle settings
cloud: aws
principle: reliability
severity: MEDIUM
rationale: |
  A usage plan without throttle_settings has no burst or steady-state rate
  ceiling for any API key attached to it. A valid but leaked, shared, or
  over-provisioned key can then issue unbounded requests, leading to backend
  overload, inflated Lambda/integration costs, or a de facto denial of
  service against other tenants of the same API. Rate limiting is the
  primary control a usage plan exists to provide; omitting it defeats the
  purpose of gating access behind an API key.
message: |
  aws_api_gateway_usage_plan does not define a throttle_settings block, so
  requests made with any associated API key are not rate-limited.
remediation: |
  Add a throttle_settings block to the usage plan:

    resource "aws_api_gateway_usage_plan" "example" {
      name = var.name

      api_stages {
        api_id = aws_api_gateway_rest_api.api.id
        stage  = aws_api_gateway_deployment.stage_api.id
      }

      throttle_settings {
        burst_limit = 100
        rate_limit  = 50
      }
    }
resources:
  aws_api_gateway_usage_plan:
    path: throttle_settings
    not_null: true
